tasks.chapterTitle: French at Boise State and Phonetics

messages.content:
Based on the provided curriculum overview for *Français inclusif* (specifically Module 02: "Me voici!"), here is a summary of the key learning objectives and content for this section:

### **Module 02: Me voici! (Intermediate/Beginner II)**

**Core Objective:**
The goal of this module is to enable students to compare and contrast their university experiences with those of students from various Francophone cultures, while developing the ability to share personal identity and family information.

**Key Learning Outcomes (Can-Do Statements):**
*   **Interpersonal:** Ask questions to gather personal information and discuss family/friends.
*   **Communication:** Describe family members, personal pastimes, and the pastimes of others.
*   **Vocational/Academic:** Discuss professional goals, studies, and nationalities.
*   **Practical Skills:** Tell time (using both official and non-official formats) and identify days of the week and months of the year.
*   **Expression:** Talk about likes, dislikes, and daily activities.

**Grammar Topics Covered:**
*   **Verbs:** Conjugation of *-ER* verbs and the irregular verb *avoir*.
*   **Sentence Structure:** Forming questions and using negation.
*   **Descriptors:** Introduction to adjectives (including placement rules) and adverbs.
*   **Possession:** Using possessive determiners.
*   **Specific Usage:** Expressions with *avoir* and telling time.

**Vocabulary Themes:**
*   Identity and personal questions.
*   Family and friends.
*   Pastimes and hobbies.
*   Time and the calendar.

***

*Source: Curricular Table of Contents [S.42]*

Lesson 2: French 102 - Presentation5 slides
Welcome to French 102
  • This is an interactive textbook for French 102 beginners (p. 3).
  • Features four modules covering vocabulary, grammar, and exercises (p. 9-10).
  • Includes cultural reflections and presentational assessments (p. 10).

This document outlines *Français inclusif*, an interactive open educational resource designed for French 102 students at Boise State University.

**Purpose and Scope**
The textbook is designed to facilitate the study of French vocabulary and grammar through interactive modules. It serves as a remix of *Français interactif* (COERLL), enhanced with additional exercises, original media, and expanded cultural representations [S.9, S.11, S.12].

**Content Structure**
The course material is organized into four distinct modules (Modules 05–08), which focus on various themes:
* Module 05: Food and dining.
* Module 06: City life (focusing on Montréal, Dakar, and Pau).
* Module 07: Seasons and celebrations.
* Module 08: Home and household tasks [S.5, S.6, S.7].

Each module follows a consistent pedagogical structure consisting of:
* Vocabulary and grammar explanations.
* Interactive exercises for homework practice.
* Cultural reflection assignments.
* Presentational speaking and writing assessments [S.5, S.6, S.7, S.10].

**Additional Resources**
Beyond the core modules, the text provides foundational support, including guides on the phonetic alphabet, daily expressions, and typing French accents. The project is an Open Education Award for Excellence finalist and offers support for external instructors, including slide decks, sample syllabi, and implementation guides, provided they contact Boise State University during the academic year [S.5, S.10, S.13].

The provided text highlights the value of studying French at Boise State, emphasizing its professional and personal benefits.

**Key Benefits of Learning French**

Career Advancement: Proficiency in French is described as a critical asset for success in the business and technology sectors (p. 14, 15).

Global Relevance: Data suggests French is a language of the future, bolstered by its widespread use in international diplomacy, the internet, and its prevalence in sub-Saharan Africa (p. 15).

Personal Enrichment: Beyond professional utility, the language enhances appreciation for various cultural fields, including history, literature, art, and gastronomy (p. 15).

Educational Resources: The program incorporates interactive learning tools, such as the phonetic alphabet ($L'alphabet phonétique$), to aid students in mastering the language (p. 16).

This document provides a foundational list of useful French expressions categorized to assist learners in classroom communication and daily interactions.

Key areas covered include:

Communication Challenges and Clarification
Students are provided with phrases to express confusion or a need for assistance, such as "J'ai une question" (I have a question), "Je ne comprends pas" (I don't understand), and "Je ne sais pas" (I don't know). To improve comprehension, learners can request "Répétez, s’il vous plaît" (Repeat, please) or ask someone to "Parlez plus lentement" (Speak more slowly), with specific variations provided for both formal and informal registers (pp. 17-21).

Language Inquiry
The text outlines how to ask for vocabulary and meanings, specifically "Comment dit-on '... ' en français?" (How do you say '...' in French?) and "Qu'est-ce que '...' veut dire?" (What does '...' mean?) (p. 22).

Instructional Commands
A series of imperative verbs are listed to help students navigate classroom activities. These include individual commands like "Écoutez" (Listen), "Lisez" (Read), and "Regardez" (Watch), as well as collective versions such as "Écoutons" (Let's listen), "Lisons" (Let's read), and "Regardons" (Let's watch) (pp. 25-27).

Spatial and Social Phrases
The guide includes practical phrases for social etiquette, such as "À vos souhaits" (Bless you) used after sneezes, and expressions for making mistakes or joking. Additionally, it covers spatial orientation with terms like "à gauche" (on the left), "à droite" (on the right), "en haut" (at the top), "en bas" (at the bottom), and "au milieu" (in the middle) (pp. 23, 28).

This section provides a guide on how to type French accents on various devices, along with attribution and credit information for the educational materials provided.

**Typing French Accents**

*   **PC Users:** Accents are created using combinations of the `ctrl` key (or `ctrl` + `shift`) followed by a designated trigger key (such as the apostrophe, backtick, or comma) and then the target letter [S.30, S.31, S.32].
*   **Mac Users:** Accents are generated by holding the `alt` key while pressing a specific letter key associated with the desired accent, followed by the target letter [S.32, S.33].
*   **Alternative Methods:** For all users in programs like Word or Google Docs, the "Insert Symbol" feature can be used. Furthermore, for mobile devices, users can press and hold a letter key to view and select available accent options [S.34].

**Attributions and Metadata**

*   **Content Origins:** The curriculum is based on *Français interactif* (created by COERLL), materials from Salt Lake Community College, and the text *Liberté* by Gretchen Angelo [S.36, S.37].
*   **Licensing:** Much of the content is licensed under a Creative Commons Attribution-NonCommercial-ShareAlike 4.0 International License [S.36, S.37].
*   **Media and Audio:** Photos, illustrations, and audio recordings used in the workbook are either openly licensed or used under the Code of Best Practices in Fair Use for Open Educational Resources. Specific credits for cover photos and audio sources (such as Forvo.com) are provided to acknowledge the original creators [S.38, S.39].

This section of the curriculum (Modules 12–16) focuses on preparing students to navigate professional, personal, and global environments within the Francophone world.

Key themes and objectives include:

Professional and Life Planning
Students explore career paths, working conditions in various Francophone countries, and the development of professional skills. Instruction covers the use of the conditional tense for making hypotheses and superlatives for workplace comparisons [S.48]. Additionally, the curriculum addresses financial literacy, such as budgeting and leasing, alongside the role of relationships in daily life [S.49].

Logistics and Travel
Modules introduce practical skills for navigating transportation systems, handling travel complications, and choosing environmentally responsible travel options. Grammatical focus shifts toward the subjunctive mood, the conditional perfect, and the pluperfect to discuss hypothetical or complex travel scenarios [S.50].

Environmental and Global Responsibility
Students learn to articulate the causes and impacts of climate change, discuss natural disasters, and advocate for sustainable living. The language focus includes advanced use of the subjunctive (for obligation, desire, or doubt) and relative pronouns such as lequel and dont [S.51].

Art and Cultural Expression
The final module emphasizes the reception and creation of art, music, and digital media across Francophone cultures. Students learn to compare artistic traditions and entertainment preferences. The grammar concludes with complex structures, including the past subjunctive and indirect discourse [S.52].

Summary of Key Linguistic Structures
Throughout these modules, students transition from foundational conversations to advanced syntax. Major grammatical milestones include:
- The conditional ($Le \ conditionnel$) used for hypothesis and politeness [S.49].
- The subjunctive ($Le \ subjonctif$) used to express necessity, doubt, and emotion [S.50, S.51, S.52].
- Advanced relative pronouns and indirect speech to describe and narrate complex ideas [S.51, S.52].

This module, titled "Bon appétit!", focuses on food-related vocabulary and communication skills in a French-speaking context. 

Key learning components include:

1. Communication Goals: Students will learn to discuss meals, personal food preferences, eating habits (frequency), and quantities. They will also practice identifying popular Francophone foods and asking questions on diverse topics (p. [S.55]).

2. Practical Applications:
- Interpretive: Analyzing French shopping lists and meal plans (p. [S.55]).
- Interpersonal: Collaborating to plan a themed upscale picnic (p. [S.55]).
- Presentational: Creating a blog post to advertise a picnic catering business (p. [S.55], [S.56]).

3. Technical Skills: The module emphasizes 21st-century skills, including the use of digital communication tools like Pressbooks and Vocaroo, and effective articulation of ideas (p. [S.56]).

4. Vocabulary: The section provides a foundational list of produce and grocery items. The terminology covers a wide variety of fruits (such as strawberries and grapes) and vegetables (such as asparagus, spinach, and potatoes), as well as culinary locations like the butcher shop (p. [S.60]).

The provided text details the curriculum structure and topics covered in Module 06, titled "La ville" (The City), which spans pages 139 to 211. This module is divided into three primary parts designed to provide a comprehensive look at urban life in French-speaking regions:

1. Montréal: The first part focuses on vocabulary (p. 139), grammar explanations (p. 142), and practice exercises (p. 152) centered on the city of Montréal.

2. Dakar: The second part shifts focus to the city of Dakar, offering specific vocabulary (p. 158), grammar instruction (p. 162), and exercises (p. 172) related to this context.

3. Se déplacer à Pau: The final part concentrates on transportation and movement within the city of Pau. It includes relevant vocabulary (p. 185), grammar rules (p. 188), and exercises (p. 200).

In addition to these core components, Module 06 concludes with an assessment for both written production and oral expression (p. 206), as well as a cultural reflection section (p. 211) intended to deepen the students' understanding of the material.

### Summary of Module 7: Les fêtes (pp. 217-272)

Module 7 focuses on vocabulary, grammar, and cultural reflections related to holidays and seasons throughout the year. The module is divided into three thematic parts:

**1. Spring and Summer (pp. 217-231)**
This section introduces vocabulary related to the spring and summer seasons. It covers the necessary grammatical structures for discussing these times of year and provides practice exercises to reinforce student learning.

**2. Autumn (pp. 232-248)**
The second part transitions to the fall season. It provides specific vocabulary for autumn activities and climate, paired with relevant grammatical explanations and exercises designed to build proficiency in describing this season.

**3. Winter (pp. 249-271)**
The final thematic section centers on winter. It includes vocabulary, grammar, and exercises tailored to winter-related topics. The section concludes with an assessment of written and oral production, followed by a cultural reflection assignment.

Each part of the module follows a consistent pedagogical structure, including vocabulary acquisition, grammar explanation, and interactive practice. The module culminates in a comprehensive cultural reflection (p. 272) to connect language learning with broader cultural contexts.

The document provided covers Module 08: "Chez moi," spanning pages 277 through 335. This module focuses on three primary subject areas related to home life:

First, the section on "à la maison" (at home) introduces vocabulary associated with the household, followed by a grammar explanation and a series of exercises to reinforce learning (p. 277–288). 

Second, the module transitions to "des tâches ménagères" (household chores), providing relevant vocabulary, grammar rules, and practice exercises for students (p. 293–305). 

Third, the content addresses the theme of "La fête" (the party or celebration). This section includes dedicated vocabulary, grammatical structures applicable to this context, and corresponding practice exercises (p. 312–327).

The module concludes with three additional components: a Cultural Reflection assignment (p. 334), an evaluation dedicated to written production (p. 335), and content supporting the overall development of language skills within these specific domestic and social contexts.
Here is a quiz for this lesson.

questions.question: If you are struggling to hear your instructor during a lesson, which of the following is the most appropriate formal request?
options: Parlez plus lentement.
J'oublie.
Je ne comprends pas.
Parlez plus fort.
questions.explanation: "Parlez plus fort" translates to "Speak more loudly," which is the correct request when you cannot hear what is being said.

questions.question: What is the primary difference in the procedure for typing accented characters between a PC and a Mac, based on the provided instructions?
options: There is no difference; both use the exact same key combinations for all accents.
PCs require holding the 'Alt' key, while Macs require holding the 'Ctrl' key.
PCs require holding the 'Ctrl' key for the first stroke, while Macs use the 'Alt' key.
Only Macs can produce accents; PC users must insert them as symbols.
questions.explanation: According to the guide, PC users use the 'Ctrl' key for the initial stroke, whereas Mac users use the 'Alt' key.

questions.question: According to the textbook introduction, what is the main purpose of the 'Allez plus Loin' (Go further) sections found in each module?
options: To serve as a self-graded practice exercise for homework.
To provide additional content beyond the core modules.
To provide the primary vocabulary list for the module.
To offer grading rubrics for the writing assessments.
questions.explanation: The introduction states that the 'Allez plus Loin' page contains additional content that expands upon the module themes.

questions.question: If you want to ask your instructor, 'What does this mean?' in French, which phrase should you use?
options: Je n'ai pas entendu.
Comment dit-on... en français?
Qu'est-ce que ... veut dire?
Je fais une erreur.
questions.explanation: "Qu'est-ce que... veut dire?" is the specific phrase provided in the Useful Expressions chart to ask for the meaning of a word or phrase, whereas "Comment dit-on" is used to ask how to say something.

questions.question: In the context of the 'Français inclusif' textbook, what do the three parts of each module contain?
options: Vocabulary, grammar explanation, and exercises.
A cultural reflection, an audio recording, and a quiz.
A grammar explanation, a cultural reading, and a video.
An introduction, an assessment, and a bibliography.
questions.explanation: The introduction specifies that each module is composed of three parts: Vocabulary (Le vocabulaire), Grammar explanations (Explication de grammaire), and Exercises (Les exercices).

cards.front: How do you say 'I have a question' in French?
How do you say 'I don't understand' in French?
How do you say 'I don't know' in French?
How do you say 'Repeat, please' in French (informal)?
How do you say 'Speak more slowly' in French (formal)?
How do you say 'I made a mistake' in French?
How do you type the accent 'ç' (la cédille) on a PC?
cards.back: Press the comma key (,) then the letter c.

cards.front: What does 'Je plaisante!' mean in English?
How do you say 'Write, please' in French?
What are the three parts that make up each Module in this textbook?
cards.back: 1. Le vocabulaire, 2. Explication de grammaire, 3. Les exercices.

slides.title: Welcome to French 102
bullets: This is an interactive textbook for French 102 beginners (p. 3).
Features four modules covering vocabulary, grammar, and exercises (p. 9-10).
Includes cultural reflections and presentational assessments (p. 10).
slides.notes: Welcome to your second-level French course. This textbook combines core language skills with interactive activities for a comprehensive learning experience.

slides.title: Module 5: Bon appétit!
bullets: Focuses on food procurement and preparation (p. 45-53).
Covers specialized vocabulary for grocery and dining (p. 49).
Grammar includes partitive articles and expressions of quantity (p. 5).
Learn to discuss eating habits and popular French cuisine (p. 55).
slides.notes: In this module, we explore the world of French food. You will learn to express quantities and discuss what you eat daily.

slides.title: Module 6: La ville
bullets: Explores urban life in Montréal, Dakar, and Pau (p. 139).
Practice directions and city navigation vocabulary (p. 185).
Grammar focus: Passé composé and regular -re verbs (p. 142).
Covers demonstrative determiners and travel-related language (p. 6).
slides.notes: We are heading to the city! This module focuses on describing urban landscapes and talking about past events during your travels.

slides.title: Module 7: Les fêtes
bullets: Discuss seasons, weather, and festive vocabulary (p. 217).
Organized by spring, summer, autumn, and winter (p. 6-7).
Grammar includes conjugation of verbs like 'faire' (p. 221).
Teaches descriptive language for seasonal activities (p. 221).
slides.notes: Celebrate the seasons with Module 7, where we look at how weather and holidays influence daily life and language.

slides.title: Module 8: Chez moi
bullets: Covers household chores and home environments (p. 277).
Includes vocabulary for hosting parties and celebrations (p. 312).
Focuses on daily routine and household management grammar (p. 280).
Provides assessments for writing and cultural reflection (p. 335).
slides.notes: Finally, we return home to look at domestic life, chores, and the art of hosting others in your personal space.
